text: The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide adhesions between the colon and the abdominal wall. Subsequently, the bladder is dissected posteriorly, freeing it from the anterior abdominal wall and providing access to the retropubic space, or Retzius space. The bladder is mobilized to facilitate a tension-free closure.

The surgeon performs a cystostomy on the bladder dome, dissecting the suspicious area with safety margins using an ultrasonic scalpel. The area is visible upon opening the bladder, with a Foley catheter present. Vesicle closure is achieved with a running suture in two separate planes of mucosa and musculature using a 2-0 Vicryl suture, performed totally intracorporeally.
The surgeon closes the second plane of the bladder wall with a running suture using V-lock, tying the two threads together at the end of the procedure.

The surgeon performs a leak check using saline solution, followed by mobilization of the sigmoid colon through lysing of omental adhesions to the abdominal wall. The sigmoidectomy is completed while preserving the left colic artery. The proximal rectum is divided using an endogea stapler, and tissue perfusion is assessed with indocyanine green to determine the optimal site for bowel division. An end-to-end circular anastomosis is performed, and a low-pressure suction drain is placed in the pelvis. The clip concludes with a normal cystoscopy three weeks post-operation.
